component.name: Loft
component.summary: Desktop integration for messaging apps on Linux

description.p: Loft provides full desktop integration for WhatsApp, Facebook Messenger, Slack and Telegram on Linux, including voice and video calling, system tray icons, badge counts, and close-to-tray behavior.
Unlike Electron wrappers, Loft uses your real Google Chrome installation for full WebRTC and codec support, whilst a backend daemon ensures functional system integration on both GNOME and KDE Plasma.

screenshot.caption: Loft Manager showing available services (dark mode)
WhatsApp service during a video call

ul.li: The panel/tray icon now reliably shows up at login when multiple services autostart.
Hide-to-tray from the Loft titlebar now works when Loft and Chrome are both installed as Flatpaks.
Clicking a service from a different workspace brings its window to you, instead of showing a "ready" notification and throwing you back to the original workspace.
The dock now shows a proper icon for each service (Messenger, WhatsApp, Slack, Telegram) instead of a single Chrome icon. Alt-tabbing to a service also correctly raises its window.
Messenger notifications now include emoji that were part of the message.
The Loft titlebar slides in smoothly on Telegram instead of stuttering.
Links to Facebook itself (profiles, posts, photos…) inside a Messenger conversation now open in your default browser instead of replacing the conversation.
Logging out with Loft services running no longer produces "Chrome has crashed" notifications on your next login.
Loft now shows an unread count in GNOME's Background Apps list, so you can see at a glance how many messages are waiting without opening the app.
Desktop notifications now appear when a Loft window is open but not focused (for example, hidden behind another app). Previously they only appeared when the window was minimised or closed to tray.

description.p: Initial release with WhatsApp, Messenger, Slack and Telegram support.
